entities.name: Vengeance of Troy (Vindicta Troie)

attributes.purpose: cause hatred, bodily harm, or death

entities.raw_quote: A fifteenth-century Liber de angelis [...] contains an experiment called the Vindicta Troie (Vengeance of Troy), which can be used to arouse hatred or to cause bodily harm or even death.

entities.name: Liber de angelis

attributes.contents: contains Vindicta Troie and techniques for discord

entities.raw_quote: A fifteenth-century Liber de angelis [...] contains an experiment called the Vindicta Troie

entities.name: Munich Handbook (CLM 849)

attributes.contents: contains psychological experiments, no. 2 and no. 5 for harm

entities.raw_quote: The psychological experiments in the Munich handbook are generally much longer [...] Even of all the experiments in the Munich manual, only two (nos 2 and 5), are designed specifically and exclusively to cause harm

entities.name: Picatrix
entities.raw_quote: astral magic translated into Latin from Arabic sources such as Picatrix [...] recipes for madness in Picatrix

attributes.contents: experiments using images to channel astrological power

entities.raw_quote: Thabit ibn Qurra’s De imaginibus [...] experiments for such goals as destroying a city or region, becoming ruler of a place

entities.name: SATOR-AREPO square
entities.raw_quote: One fifteenth-century compilation prescribes writing out the SATOR-AREPO square on virgin parchment with the blood of a white dove

entities.name: human bones and hair
entities.raw_quote: The image should be fumigated with various substances, including human bones and hair

entities.name: funeral cloth
entities.raw_quote: wrapped in a funeral cloth and buried

entities.name: candle (funeral)
entities.raw_quote: made of wax, preferably from candles used at a funeral

entities.name: iron stylus
entities.raw_quote: you should have an iron stylus, and on his forehead carve the name ‘Dyacom’

entities.name: silver or tin (for casting)
entities.raw_quote: on the first hour of Sunday under a waxing moon take silver or tin and melt it over a fire

entities.name: cinnamon, long pepper, agrimony
entities.raw_quote: fumigate his image with these aromatics – cinnamon, long pepper and the herb agrimony

entities.name: iron chain
entities.raw_quote: take a small iron chain and bind it to the neck of his image

entities.name: linen cloth
entities.raw_quote: have a clean linen cloth, and wrap the image in it

entities.name: black hen’s egg

attributes.use: used by Riccola di Puccio to sever love

entities.raw_quote: using magic to bring disaffection between a husband and wife; she recited charms [...] over an egg from a black hen

entities.name: female cat
entities.raw_quote: then he cut the egg in half and gave one part to a female cat

entities.name: male dog

attributes.use: receives other half of divided egg

entities.raw_quote: and the other to a male dog

summary: This chunk details psychological experiments from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), focusing on harm-inducing rituals (nos. 2 and 5) that use sympathetic magic—e.g., stones, images, burial—and invoke demonic spirits like Mirael or antipathetic names (Cartutay–Geteritacon; Puzanil–Mansator). It contrasts these with a more elaborate favor-gaining ritual (no. 4), which inscribes eleven demonic names on tin images and binds them symbolically. The text also references parallel traditions in the Liber de angelis (e.g., Vindicta Troie) and Picatrix, highlighting the Munich Handbook’s preference for imitative over natural-ingredient magic.
key_passages: On one you must carve the name of one person, with the names Cartutay, Momabel, Sobil and Geteritacon. On the other [you must carve] the name of the other person and the names Puzanil, Pimaton, Folfitoy, and Mansator. These names are highly antipathetic to each other.

I conjure you most inimical spirits, by the glory of the everlasting God, to sow and arouse as much hatred between so-and-so and so-and-so, whose names are carved here on these stones, as there is between you.

O Dyacon, Pumeon, Termines, Peripaos, Midain, Fabin, Gebel, Dorayl, Vmeloth, Tereoth and Bireoth, most benevolent spirits who sow concord [...] that by this image made in the name of so-and-so you may bind him to me in such a manner that he will revere me above all mortals.

The psychological experiments in the Munich handbook are generally much longer and more fully developed than the materials in these Arabic sources. What they add are chiefly conjurations and operations of sympathetic magic.
Even of all the experiments in the Munich manual, only two (nos 2 and 5), are designed specifically and exclusively to cause harm, and the damage is specifically psychological
